description:
You are a build-engineer who has been asked to sanity-check an old release
    package before it is forwarded to QA.

    A legacy tarball is already present at:

        /home/user/old_releases/release_v1.tar.gz

    Your job consists of two simple steps:

    1.  Extract the tarball in place so that the directory
            /home/user/old_releases/release_v1
        is recreated with its full file hierarchy.

    2.  Produce an alphabetical inventory of every regular file
        inside that directory tree (directories themselves must
        NOT be listed).  Save this inventory to

        /home/user/build_logs/release_v1_filelist.log

        Output-format requirements for the log file
        (these are *exactly* what the automated tests will check):

        • One path per line, **no leading or trailing spaces**.  
        • Paths must be **relative to /home/user/old_releases**,  
          i.e. they must all begin with “release_v1/”.  
        • Pure ASCII, UTF-8 encoded, with UNIX line endings “\n”.  
        • Lines sorted in strict byte-wise lexical order  
          (as produced by the default `sort` utility).  
        • Do NOT include directories, only regular file paths.  
        • The file must end with a trailing newline after the last entry.

    Example of a correctly formatted log file (the real file list
    may differ, this is only to show structure):

        release_v1/LICENSE
        release_v1/README.md
        release_v1/bin/run.sh
        release_v1/src/main.c
        ← there is a newline right here

    Hints:
      • All required tools are available in a typical POSIX shell environment.
      • The whole task can be completed with roughly two or three commands.
      • Make sure the parent directory /home/user/build_logs exists before
        writing the log file.

    Once these two actions are done and the log file meets the exact
    formatting rules above, the task is complete.
truth:
Initial filesystem state BEFORE the agent starts:

    1. Directory /home/user/old_releases exists (0755 user:user).
    2. File /home/user/old_releases/release_v1.tar.gz exists.  It is a valid
       gzip-compressed tar archive containing the following regular files
       with 0644 permissions (all timestamps ignored for grading):

        • release_v1/LICENSE        – ASCII text, content: “Legacy License\n”
        • release_v1/README.md      – ASCII text, content: “# Release v1\n”
        • release_v1/bin/run.sh     – ASCII text, content: “#!/bin/sh\necho ok\n”
        • release_v1/src/main.c     – ASCII text, minimal C “int main(){return 0;}”

       The tarball also embeds the directories
       release_v1/, release_v1/bin/, and release_v1/src/.

    3. Directory /home/user/build_logs does NOT yet exist.

    Expected final state AFTER the agent finishes:

    1. Directory /home/user/old_releases/release_v1 has been extracted and now
       exists in the filesystem with the same hierarchy and files listed above.

    2. Directory /home/user/build_logs has been created (0755 user:user).

    3. File /home/user/build_logs/release_v1_filelist.log exists (0644
       user:user) and contains **exactly**:

        release_v1/LICENSE
        release_v1/README.md
        release_v1/bin/run.sh
        release_v1/src/main.c
        \n (ASCII 0x0A newline at EOF)

       There are no extra spaces, blank lines, or carriage returns.

    Test script will:

       a. Verify extraction by checking that each of the four regular files
          above exists on disk with non-zero size.

       b. Read /home/user/build_logs/release_v1_filelist.log and assert that
          the content matches the four-line string (including final newline)
          in the exact order shown.

    No other files or directories will be checked or created by the test
    suite.
